title: Wet-felted vessel over a balloon resist
subtitle: Using a blown balloon as a temporary form to shape a round felt bowl or pod
excerpt: Wrapping roving around a blown-up balloon and felting it in place produces a hollow vessel when the balloon is removed. The method is forgiving for beginners because the balloon holds the roving in a consistent curved shape during the initial rubbing phase. Finished vessels work as small bowls, hanging planters, or storage pots.
sourceNotes: Balloon-resist wet felting is a standard beginner technique in contemporary craft instruction. The underlying felting chemistry (wool fibre scales, heat, soap, agitation) is observation-led and consistent with the Victorian felting tradition.

glossaryTerms.definition: An object placed inside or under the roving during wet felting to create a defined shape or an opening in the finished piece. Balloons, foam sheets, and bubble wrap are common resists.
The stage of wet felting after initial fibre cohesion, when the piece is worked more aggressively to shrink and densify the felt. Rolling, throwing, and kneading all full the piece.

content.text: A balloon inflated to roughly 15 cm diameter (slightly smaller than a grapefruit) makes a vessel around 10 to 12 cm wide once deflated and felted. The balloon acts as a
: the felt forms around it, and when the balloon is removed, the hollow shape remains. A small opening at the top is left unsealed or cut open after felting.

attrs.title: Needle safety
attrs.body: Test water temperature on the back of your wrist before submerging hands. 60°C water stings; allow it to cool to 50°C for comfortable working.

items.name: Felting net or fine-mesh net curtain

content.text: Pull roving into thin wisps. Lay them across the top half of the balloon in one direction, covering the dome evenly with a single layer. Apply a second layer perpendicular to the first. Apply a third layer at 45 degrees. Three crossed layers at this stage give the vessel enough body to hold shape after the balloon is removed.
Step 2: Wet and rub the first side

Wet the roving by drizzling hot soapy water from a cup. Cover with the felting net. Press and rub gently in circular motions for 5 minutes until the fibres begin to mat. The surface should hold together when you lift a corner of the net. Do not rub hard at this stage.
Step 3: Turn and cover the underside

Carefully turn the balloon over. Tuck the roving from the felted top half around the base of the balloon, folding the edges inward. Apply fresh layers over the bottom half to match the top. Leave a small circle (4 to 5 cm diameter) uncovered at the very bottom of the balloon; this is where you will release the balloon later. Wet, net, and rub for another 5 minutes.

Remove the net. Massage the whole surface for 10 to 15 minutes using firm, consistent circular pressure. Dip the vessel in cold water, then back in hot soapy water to shock the fibres. The felt will tighten around the balloon.
by rolling the balloon on the bamboo mat for 5 minutes in each direction.
Step 5: Remove the balloon and shape

When the felt is firm enough to hold its shape unaided (the surface should not tear when pinched), deflate the balloon by untying the knot at the neck. Ease the balloon out through the opening. Reshape the vessel by hand while the felt is still damp. Place it open-side down on a towel to dry.

The top opening is naturally uneven where the roving ends were folded over. Trim the edge to a neat circle with sharp scissors once the vessel is dry. Alternatively, leave the irregular edge as a design feature: many basket-style vessels look intentional with an organic rim.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Three common problems with balloon-resist vessels.

items.symptom: Felt tears when the balloon is removed
items.cause: Insufficient fulling. The felt surface has matted but not yet bonded into a self-supporting shell.
items.fix: Re-inflate the balloon (or fill with crumpled newspaper) and continue rubbing for 10 more minutes before trying again.

items.symptom: Vessel collapses flat after the balloon is removed
items.cause: Felt is thin in some areas due to uneven roving layers.
items.fix: Patch thin areas with small wisps of fresh roving while damp, then wet-felt them in by rubbing. Stuff with a balled-up tea towel and allow to dry in shape.

items.symptom: Opening at the top is too small to remove the balloon
items.cause: Roving was extended all the way to the top, closing the opening during felting.
items.fix: Cut a small opening at the top with sharp scissors once the felt is firm. The felt will not unravel if it is sufficiently fulled.
